schools.summary: The last plague announced: 'and the LORD said unto Moses, Yet will I bring one plague more upon Pharaoh, and upon Egypt; afterwards he will let you go hence: when he shall let you go, he shall surely thrust you out hence altogether' — God announcing to Moses (perhaps in a flashback, or before the final interview) that one last plague would come (the death of the firstborn, vv.4-6), after which Pharaoh would let Israel go (the climax of the judgments, the assurance of the deliverance now at hand); after this plague Pharaoh would let them go — the deliverance assured (the end of the contest, the last judgment to break the king's obstinacy); 'he shall surely thrust you out hence altogether' (Pharaoh, so far from refusing, would now thrust Israel out, drive them out in haste, Exodus 12:31-33, the complete reversal, the king who would not let them go now urging them out, 'altogether,' wholly, all the people and substance, the providence, God's strong hand, Exodus 6:1, forcing the king) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, JFB.

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, Yet will I bring one plague more upon Pharaoh, and upon Egypt; afterwards he will let you go hence... he shall surely thrust you out hence altogether
the last plague announced
the death of the firstborn to break the king's obstinacy (vv.4-6)
the complete reversal - Pharaoh thrusting Israel out (Exodus 6:1; 12:31-33)

schools.summary: The command to ask the Egyptians for jewels: 'speak now in the ears of the people, and let every man borrow of his neighbour, and every woman of her neighbour, jewels of silver, and jewels of gold' — God commanding the Israelites to ask of their Egyptian neighbours jewels of silver and gold (and raiment, Exodus 12:35), the 'borrow' (Hebrew shaal, to ask, request — not deceitfully borrowing with intent to defraud, but asking as gifts, which the Egyptians freely gave, Exodus 12:36, the spoiling of Egypt foretold, Genesis 15:14, 'afterward shall they come out with great substance'); 'borrow' means 'ask' (request) — the Israelites asking, the Egyptians giving (God giving them favor, v.3; Exodus 12:36, not a fraudulent borrowing, the older objection answered, but a free asking and free giving, the just recompense, the wages of long unpaid bondage, the spoiling of the oppressor, Genesis 15:14); 'jewels of silver, and jewels of gold' (the wealth of Egypt given to Israel, the spoiling of the Egyptians, Exodus 12:36, the recompense for the bondage, the substance promised to Abraham, Genesis 15:14, the providence, God enriching his people at the Exodus, the gold and silver later used for the tabernacle, Exodus 35:5) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: Does 'borrow' imply the Israelites defrauded the Egyptians?

sides.position: No - the Hebrew (shaal) means 'ask, request'; the Israelites asked, the Egyptians freely gave (God giving them favor, v.3; Exodus 12:36); a free asking and free giving, the just recompense for the long unpaid bondage (the spoiling foretold, Genesis 15:14)

themes: Speak now in the ears of the people, and let every man borrow of his neighbour, and every woman of her neighbour, jewels of silver, and jewels of gold
the command to ask the Egyptians for jewels
'borrow' means 'ask' - a free asking and free giving, not fraud (Exodus 12:36)
the just recompense for the bondage, the spoiling foretold (Genesis 15:14)

schools.summary: God giving Israel favor and Moses' greatness: 'and the LORD gave the people favour in the sight of the Egyptians. Moreover the man Moses was very great in the land of Egypt, in the sight of Pharaoh's servants, and in the sight of the people' — God giving the Israelites favor with the Egyptians, so that they freely gave the jewels (v.2; Exodus 12:36, the providence, God inclining the hearts of the Egyptians to give, the spoiling accomplished by favor, not fraud, the LORD turning the oppressors' hearts); Moses 'very great' (held in awe and honor) in Egypt — in the sight of Pharaoh's servants and the people (the greatness of the prophet, the plagues having made his name terrible and honored, the providence, Moses exalted, that his word might prevail); 'in the sight of Pharaoh's servants, and in the sight of the people' (Moses honored by all, even the Egyptians, the servants and the people, though Pharaoh hardened, the awe of the prophet, the providence, the man of God great in the land, the deliverance advancing) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott.

themes: And the LORD gave the people favour in the sight of the Egyptians. Moreover the man Moses was very great in the land of Egypt, in the sight of Pharaoh's servants, and in the sight of the people
God giving Israel favor and Moses' greatness
God turning the oppressors' hearts to give (Exodus 12:36)
the prophet honored by all, though Pharaoh hardened

schools.summary: Moses announcing the LORD's coming at midnight: 'and Moses said, Thus saith the LORD, About midnight will I go out into the midst of Egypt' — Moses (likely still before Pharaoh, ch. 10:29, delivering the final word, or to the people) announcing the last plague: about midnight the LORD himself would go out into the midst of Egypt to smite the firstborn (v.5, the solemn announcement of the LORD's coming in judgment, the awful threat); the midnight hour (the dead of night, the time of the judgment, Exodus 12:29) — the appointed time (the solemnity of the midnight stroke, the terror of the night); 'will I go out into the midst of Egypt' (the LORD himself coming in judgment, going through Egypt, by the destroying angel, Exodus 12:23, the immediate hand of God, the solemnity, God himself the executor of the last plague, the providence, the judgment that would break Pharaoh and free Israel) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: And Moses said, Thus saith the LORD, About midnight will I go out into the midst of Egypt
Moses announcing the LORD's coming at midnight
the solemn midnight stroke, the appointed hour (Exodus 12:29)
the LORD himself coming in judgment, by the destroying angel (Exodus 12:23)

schools.summary: The death of the firstborn announced: 'and all the firstborn in the land of Egypt shall die, from the firstborn of Pharaoh that sitteth upon his throne, even unto the firstborn of the maidservant that is behind the mill; and all the firstborn of beasts' — the great threat — 'all the firstborn in the land of Egypt shall die': the tenth and last plague — every firstborn in Egypt to die (the eldest son of every family, the hope and stay of the house, and the firstborn of the beasts, the immediate, universal stroke of death, by the LORD's own hand, the destroying angel, Exodus 12:23, 29, the judgment on Egypt's firstborn, a recompense, the witnesses note, for Pharaoh's slaying of Israel's sons, Exodus 1:22, and Israel being God's firstborn, Exodus 4:22-23, 'let my son go... if thou refuse, I will slay thy son, even thy firstborn,' the climax of the judgments); the sweep of the judgment — from the highest (Pharaoh's firstborn, the crown prince, the heir) to the lowest (the maidservant's firstborn, behind the mill, none exempt, the great and the mean alike, the universal stroke, the leveling judgment of God, no rank or station spared); 'and all the firstborn of beasts' (even the firstborn of the cattle smitten, the judgment on the beasts the Egyptians worshipped, Exodus 12:12, 'against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgment,' the complete judgment, the providence, the last plague breaking Pharaoh, freeing Israel, ch. 12) — Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the death of the firstborn signify?

sides.position: The tenth and climactic judgment, the eldest of every house smitten by the LORD's own hand (Exodus 12:23, 29) - a recompense for Pharaoh's slaying of Israel's sons (Exodus 1:22) and the threatened doom for refusing God's firstborn (Exodus 4:22-23); the universal stroke, from highest to lowest, the firstborn of the beasts and Egypt's gods judged (Exodus 12:12)

themes: And all the firstborn in the land of Egypt shall die, from the firstborn of Pharaoh that sitteth upon his throne, even unto the firstborn of the maidservant that is behind the mill; and all the firstborn of beasts
the death of the firstborn announced (the crux of the last plague)
the climactic judgment, the eldest of every house smitten by the LORD's hand (Exodus 12:23, 29)
a recompense for the slaying of Israel's sons (Exodus 1:22), the doom for refusing God's firstborn (Exodus 4:22-23; 12:12)

schools.summary: The great cry of Egypt foretold: 'and there shall be a great cry throughout all the land of Egypt, such as there was none like it, nor shall be like it any more' — the death of the firstborn (v.5) would raise a great cry (of grief and anguish) throughout Egypt, unequalled before or after (the universal mourning, every house bereaved, Exodus 12:30, the terror and grief of the judgment, the unequalled lamentation); the great cry (the universal wail of grief, every house having its dead, Exodus 12:30) — the anguish of the judgment (the terror of the bereaved land); 'such as there was none like it, nor shall be like it any more' (the cry unequalled, the greatest mourning Egypt ever knew, the magnitude of the judgment, the providence, the cry that would break Pharaoh's obstinacy, Exodus 12:31) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And there shall be a great cry throughout all the land of Egypt, such as there was none like it, nor shall be like it any more
the great cry of Egypt foretold
the universal mourning, every house bereaved (Exodus 12:30)
the unequalled anguish breaking the king (Exodus 12:31)

schools.summary: God's difference between Egypt and Israel: 'but against any of the children of Israel shall not a dog move his tongue, against man or beast: that ye may know how that the LORD doth put a difference between the Egyptians and Israel' — while Egypt mourned its dead, Israel would be so safe that not even a dog would bark (move his tongue) against them, man or beast (the perfect safety and peace of Israel, in the night of Egypt's judgment, the proverbial expression of complete security, not the least disturbance, the distinction of God's people); the figure ('not a dog move his tongue') — the perfect quiet and safety of Israel (not a sound of trouble, the peace of God's people in the night of judgment, the complete protection); 'that ye may know how that the LORD doth put a difference between the Egyptians and Israel' (the purpose: that all might know the LORD distinguishes between his people and the world, as the flies, Exodus 8:22; the darkness, Exodus 10:23, the distinction of grace, Israel safe, Egypt judged, the providence, God severing his own in the day of judgment, Malachi 3:18, the sealing of his people, Revelation 7:3) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke.

themes: But against any of the children of Israel shall not a dog move his tongue, against man or beast: that ye may know how that the LORD doth put a difference between the Egyptians and Israel
God's difference between Egypt and Israel
the perfect safety of God's people in the night of the world's judgment
the distinction of grace (Exodus 8:22; 10:23; Malachi 3:18; Revelation 7:3)

schools.summary: Moses foretelling the Egyptians' submission, and his departure in anger: 'and all these thy servants shall come down unto me, and bow down themselves unto me, saying, Get thee out, and all the people that follow thee: and after that I will go out. And he went out from Pharaoh in a great anger' — Moses foretelling that Pharaoh's own servants would come and bow before Moses, begging Israel to depart (Exodus 12:31-33, the reversal, the proud court forced to entreat the deliverers, the urgency of the Egyptians to be rid of Israel after the last plague); Moses' word ('after that I will go out') — Israel to depart after the plague and the Egyptians' entreaty (the deliverance at hand, the Exodus foretold); 'he went out from Pharaoh in a great anger' (Moses leaving Pharaoh in great, holy, righteous anger, indignation at the king's obstinacy and hardened defiance, the just wrath of the prophet, not sinful passion, but holy indignation at the contempt of God, the providence, the last interview ended, the plague to fall, ch. 12) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And all these thy servants shall come down unto me, and bow down themselves unto me, saying, Get thee out... And he went out from Pharaoh in a great anger
Moses foretelling the Egyptians' submission, and his departure in anger
the reversal - the proud court to entreat the deliverers (Exodus 12:31-33)
Moses' holy indignation at the king's defiance of God

schools.summary: The purpose of Pharaoh's refusal - the multiplying of God's wonders: 'and the LORD said unto Moses, Pharaoh shall not hearken unto you; that my wonders may be multiplied in the land of Egypt' — God telling Moses that Pharaoh would not hearken (would persist in refusing, the foreknown obstinacy, the hardening confirmed, Exodus 7:3-4, the king's persistent defiance foretold); the purpose — 'that my wonders may be multiplied in the land of Egypt': Pharaoh's refusal (his hardening) overruled by God for the multiplying of his wonders (the many signs and plagues, the great displays of his power, the providence, God's purpose in the obstinacy — that his works might be multiplied, his power shown, Exodus 7:3-4; 9:16; Romans 9:17, 'that I might shew my power in thee,' the sovereign overruling, the king's defiance serving God's glory, the deliverance made the more glorious by the many wonders, the lesson — God turns even the obstinacy of the wicked to his own ends and glory); the multiplying of the wonders (the many plagues, the signs told to generations, Exodus 10:2) — God's power displayed the more by the prolonged contest (the providence, the obstinacy overruled for the glory of God and the instruction of his people) — Calvin, Gill, Clarke.

sides.position: His foreknown obstinacy (Exodus 7:3-4) overruled by God for the multiplying of his signs and the displaying of his power (Exodus 9:16; Romans 9:17) - the deliverance made the more glorious, his works told to generations (Exodus 10:2); God turning the obstinacy of the wicked to his own glory

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, Pharaoh shall not hearken unto you; that my wonders may be multiplied in the land of Egypt
the purpose of Pharaoh's refusal - the multiplying of God's wonders
the foreknown obstinacy (Exodus 7:3-4) overruled for the displaying of God's power (Exodus 9:16; Romans 9:17)
God turning the obstinacy of the wicked to his own glory

schools.summary: The summary of the signs and Pharaoh's hardening: 'and Moses and Aaron did all these wonders before Pharaoh: and the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart, so that he would not let the children of Israel go out of his land' — the summary of the whole contest: Moses and Aaron having done all the signs and wonders (the nine plagues, the rod-serpent) before Pharaoh (the recapitulation of the judgments, the faithful execution of God's commission, the wonders all wrought); 'the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart' (the judicial hardening, the summary of the hardening through the plagues, Exodus 4:21; 7:3, following the king's self-hardening, Exodus 8:15, 32) — the divine sovereignty over the obstinate king (the hardening that occasioned the multiplied wonders, v.9); 'he would not let the children of Israel go out of his land' (the persistent refusal, through all the plagues, the obstinacy, until the last plague, ch. 12, would force the release, the providence, the hardening overruled, the wonders multiplied, the deliverance made glorious, the summary before the Passover and Exodus, ch. 12) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Moses and Aaron did all these wonders before Pharaoh: and the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart, so that he would not let the children of Israel go out of his land
the summary of the signs and Pharaoh's hardening
the recapitulation of the whole contest, the judgments wrought
the judicial hardening (Exodus 4:21; 7:3), the deliverance now at hand (ch. 12)
